full_response:
Step 1   Distinguish the two different “accelerations” one can talk about  

a) Coordinate (or 3-)acceleration  
  a ≡ d v/dt evaluated with the coordinate time t of some inertial frame.  

b) Proper acceleration  
  α ≡ |A | where A μ = dU μ/dτ is the 4-acceleration and τ is the rocket’s proper time.  
  In components     A μA μ = −α² (signature − + + +).

Step 2   What changes under a Lorentz transformation  

• 4-vectors transform with Λ; their squared length is an invariant.  
 Therefore α is the same in every inertial frame.  

• The 3-velocity transforms by the usual velocity-addition formulas; taking another
 derivative with respect to each observer’s own coordinate time gives a much more
 complicated transformation law,

  a′∥ = a∥ /γ³(1−βu∥)³ , 
  a′⊥ = (a⊥)/γ²(1−βu∥)² ,

 so d²x/dt² is definitely not invariant.

Step 3   Physical interpretation  

What an accelerometer bolted to the rocket reads is the proper acceleration α; every
inertial observer will compute the same α from the 4-acceleration.  
However, if the observers merely look at the rocket’s world-line and take two
derivatives with respect to their own coordinate time, they will in general obtain
different numbers because their notions of simultaneity and of “one second” differ.

Conclusion  

Observers in different inertial frames agree on the rocket’s proper acceleration but
do not, in general, agree on its coordinate (3-)acceleration d²x/dt² measured in their
own frames.

\boxed{\text{No.  Different inertial observers in general measure different coordinate
accelerations; only the invariant proper acceleration is the same for all of them.}}  

Confidence: 79%
extracted_answer:
\text{No.  Different inertial observers in general measure different coordinate
accelerations; only the invariant proper acceleration is the same for all of them.}
